assesment:
Melanoma In Situ - Mid Chest (WC24-00419)
Considering the patient's history, examination and histopathology, the diagnosis is most consistent with Melanoma In Situ. At this stage, MMIS is restricted to the the epidermis and requires immediate treatment to prevent the spread of the cancer.
Management options discussed with patient: Surgical Removal. 
Patient opted to proceed with the following treatment: 
-Mohs done today. Discussed the procedure with the patient and answered all of their questions. The pathology report and previous biopsy photos were reviewed and the site was confirmed with the patient. Patient verbalized understanding of the risks of surgery, including infection and scarring. Patient opted to proceed with the procedure. Patient instructed to monitor for signs of infection, including swelling, erythema, wound drainage or fever. 
-Had an informative discussion about the Mohs procedure with the patient. Reviewed with patient that Mohs surgery is a procedure to treat skin cancer that involves cutting away thin layers of the skin while sacrificing a minimum amount of normal tissue. Mohs surgery is ideal for skin cancers that have a high risk of recurrence, for areas where one would want to keep as much healthy tissue as possible, for tumors that have edges that are hard to define or are large and grow quickly. Risks of Mohs surgery include but are not limited to: bleeding, pain or tenderness at site, and possible risk of infection and large scarring. Other uncommon risks are temporary or permanent numbness and/or weakness of the surgical area.
Squamous Cell Carcinoma - Mid Chest WC24-278.
Considering the patient's history, examination and histopathology, the diagnosis is most consistent with Squamous Cell Carcinoma.
Management options discussed with patient: Mohs micrographic surgery, ED&C, excisional surgery, and cryosurgery. Risk and benefits of each option reviewed.
Patient opted to proceed with the following treatment:
-Excision done today.
-Discussed the procedure with the patient and answered all of their questions. The site and any previous pathology associated with site (if applicable) was reviewed and confirmed with the patient. Patient verbalized understanding of the risks of surgery, including infection, bleeding, tenderness at site, and scarring. Other uncommon risks including nerve damage, temporary or permanent nubmness or weakenss of the the surgical area discussed. 
Patient opted to proceed with the excision. Patient instructed to monitor for signs of infection, including swelling, erythema, wound drainage or fever.
-Wound care instructions reviewed with patient. Patient will return to office in 14 days for suture removal.
-Apply mupirocin ointment to wound TID until sutures are removed. 
- Additional fat removed from lesion. Per Rao. 
Follow up: after 10 Days for Suture removal with Attiya Haroon, MD
currentmedication: Pt will bring list next visit
biopsyNotes:
A. Excision Malignant Lesion & Margins
Clinical Diagnosis: EX SCC
Location: Mid Chest
Method: Excision
Lesion Size: 3.0 - 2.0 cm
Excision Size, including margins: 3.5 x 4.0 cm
The patient was evaluated preoperative and noted to be in stable condition. 
 Informed consent was obtained from the patient. All risks, benefits, and alternatives regarding the survey have been reviewed in detail with the patient. 
 This includes risks of bleeding, infection, scarring recurrence of lesion and need for further procedures. 
After consent was obtained, attention was turned toward the area of interest which was disinfected and cleaned with alcohol. Local anesthetic, 2% lidocaine with epinephrine was infiltrated around the site. An elliptical incision with the appropriate margins was made using a Number 15 to obtain a full thickness specimen. Dissection of the surrounding/overlying tissue was performed to isolate, expose and remove the lesion. The specimen was placed in a bottle labeled A. #WC24-542. 
Repair: Complex Closure 
The wound was closed with a complex closure on 05-06-2024. The wound size with margins is 3.5 - 4.0 cm. The wound was approximated utilizing 4-O Vicryl and Using 4-O Nylon sutures. The final closure size is 5.5 cm. 
Pressure was applied to achieve hemostasis. Bacitracin was applied at the wound site and it was bandaged. Patient tolerated the procedure well and wound care instructions were reviewed and given to the patient.
mohsNotes:
Location: Left Temple
Pre-op Diagnosis: Lentigo Maligna
Pathology Reference: WC24-00419
Mohs Log: 5686
In an informative discussion of the mohs procedure, the nature of the skin cancer, 
 alternative treatment options, consequences of no treatment and convalescence were reviewed with the patient. 
 The risks and benefits, including but not limited to: bleeding, spread of infection, pain, scarring, bruising, 
 distortion of the surrounding areas, allergic reaction to the medications used, recurrence of the tumor, 
 and opening of the wound were discussed and understood by the patient. 
All questions from the patient were answered adequately. 
 No guarantees were given and the patient was informed that Mohs surgery is indicated to provide 
 the highest cure rate by excising the tissue in multiple stages and preserving the healthy tissue as much as possible. 
After obtaining informed consent, the patient was given a mirror and 
 corroborated the location of the tumor prior to surgery. 
 Mohs Micrographic Procedure:
Attention was turned toward the surgical site, which was disinfected and cleansed with alcohol. The patient was draped using a sterile technique. Local anesthetic lidocaine 1 % with epinephrine was infiltrated around the site. The procedure was performed in accordance with the Mohs micrographic surgical technique. 
 The tumor site was debulk prior to the first Mohs stage. With each stage, a thin layer of tissue was excised with a narrow margin of normal appearing skin using the Mohs fresh tissue technique. Hemostasis was achieved with Pressure. The surgical site was dressed with gauze pressure dressings and the patient remained in the surgical area. The tissue was prepared for the cryostat and sectioned. Each section was coded, cut and stained for microscopic examination. The slides were then analyzed by the Mohs surgeon for any evidence of remaining tumor. Areas noted to be positive on the previous stage (if applicable) were removed with the mohs technique and processed for analysis. This process was repeated until a tumor free plane was obtained. 
1st Stage: 2 Sections, Negative
Post-Mohs Deficit Size: 2.2 x 2.3 cm.
 Repair Options:
 
 After a discussion of wound management options, 
 due to the size and location of the defect, and the need to achieve optimal cosmesis, 
 avoid hematoma, the following wound management was agreed upon to provide the best functional and cosmetic result. 
 Performed: 
 The wound was closed with a Complex Closure on the same day of mohs. 
 Reconstruction:
Repair: Complex Closure
The postoperative deficit was 2.2 x 2.3 cm. The wound was closed with a complex closure. The defect was undermined extensively (1 cm) to reduce tension and distortion of the adjacent structures. Buried absorbable sutures were also used to close the subcutaneous and dermal components of the defect. The wound was approximated utilizing 4-O Vicryl and 4-O Nylon sutures. 
The final closure size is 4.5 cm. 
 Post-Operative:
The patient tolerated the procedure well with no complications, and minimal bleeding. The wound was covered with Vaseline and Press Dressing. Detailed oral and written post-operative instructions were reviewed and given to the patient. The patient instructed to remove dressing in 24 hours, sleep with the surgical site elevated this evening, avoid alcohol, trauma and tobacco.
 Patient was informed to return in 10 Days for suture removal with Attiya Haroon MD.

 Considering the patients history, examination and histopathology, the diagnosis is most consistent with Lentigo Maligna. Discussed with patient that Lentigo Maligna is a type of melanoma in situ that arises from sun damages skin, particularly in areas exposed to chronic UV radiation. Treatment options for lentgo maligna aim to completely remove the lesion. Management options discussed with patient: Mohs surgery, surgical excision, malignant destruction, or topical treatments like imiquimod. Risk and benefits of each option reviewed.

 Mohs done today.

In an informative discussion of the mohs procedure, the nature of the skin cancer,

After obtaining informed consent, the patient was given a mirror and 
 corroborated the location of the tumor prior to surgery. 
 
Mohs Micrographic Procedure:
Attention was turned toward the surgical site, which was disinfected and cleansed with chlorhexidine. The patient was draped using a sterile technique. Local anesthetic lidocaine 1 % with epinephrine was infiltrated around the site. The procedure was performed in accordance with the Mohs micrographic surgical technique. 
 The tumor site was debulk prior to the first Mohs stage. With each stage, a thin layer of tissue was excised with a narrow margin of normal appearing skin using the Mohs fresh tissue technique. Hemostasis was achieved with Pressure, Electrocautery. The surgical site was dressed with gauze pressure dressings and the patient remained in the surgical area. The tissue was prepared for the cryostat and sectioned. Each section was coded, cut and stained for microscopic examination. The slides were then analyzed by the Mohs surgeon for any evidence of remaining tumor. Areas noted to be positive on the previous stage (if applicable) were removed with the mohs technique and processed for analysis. This process was repeated until a tumor free plane was obtained. 
1st Stage: 2 Sections, Negative
Post-Mohs Deficit Size: 2.1 x 1.6 cm.
 Repair Options:
 
 After a discussion of wound management options, 
 due to the size and location of the defect, and the need to achieve optimal cosmesis, 
 avoid hematoma, the following wound management was agreed upon to provide the best functional and cosmetic result. 
 Performed: 
 The wound was closed with a Complex Closure on the same day of mohs. 
 Reconstruction:
After a discussion of wound management options, due to the size and location of the defect, and the need to achieve optimal cosmesis, avoid hematoma, the following wound management was agreed upon to provide the best functional and cosmetic result.
Secondary Intention:
The wound was left to heal by secondary intention after Electrodessication for homeostasis.
The patient tolerated the procedure well with no complications, and minimal bleeding. The wound was covered with Steri Strips and Press Dressing. Detailed oral and written post-operative instructions were reviewed and given to the patient. The patient instructed to remove dressing in 24 hours, sleep with the surgical site elevated this evening, avoid alcohol, trauma and tobacco.
 Patient was informed to return in 10 Days.

Pathology Reference: WC25-01469

In an informative discussion of the mohs procedure, the nature of the skin cancer, 
 alternative treatment options, consequences of no treatment and convalescence were reviewed with the patient. 
 The risks and benefits, including but not limited to: bleeding, spread of infection, pain, scarring, bruising, 
 distortion of the surrounding areas, allergic reaction to the medications used, recurrence of the tumor, 
 and opening of the wound were discussed and understood by the patient. 
All questions from the patient were answered adequately. 
 No guarantees were given and the patient was informed that Mohs surgery is indicated to provide 
 the highest cure rate by excising the tissue in multiple stages and preserving the healthy tissue as much as possible. 
After obtaining informed consent, the patient was given a mirror and 
 corroborated the location of the tumor prior to surgery. 
 Mohs Micrographic Procedure:
Attention was turned toward the surgical site, which was disinfected and cleansed with chlorhexidine. The patient was draped using a sterile technique. Local anesthetic lidocaine 1 % with epinephrine was infiltrated around the site. The procedure was performed in accordance with the Mohs micrographic surgical technique. 
 The tumor site was debulk prior to the first Mohs stage. With each stage, a thin layer of tissue was excised with a narrow margin of normal appearing skin using the Mohs fresh tissue technique. Hemostasis was achieved with Pressure, Electrocautery. The surgical site was dressed with gauze pressure dressings and the patient remained in the surgical area. The tissue was prepared for the cryostat and sectioned. Each section was coded, cut and stained for microscopic examination. The slides were then analyzed by the Mohs surgeon for any evidence of remaining tumor. Areas noted to be positive on the previous stage (if applicable) were removed with the mohs technique and processed for analysis. This process was repeated until a tumor free plane was obtained. 
1st Stage: 2 Sections, Negative
Post-Mohs Deficit Size: 1.8 x 2.1 cm.
 Repair Options:
After a discussion of wound management options, due to the size and location of the defect, and the need to achieve optimal cosmesis, avoid hematoma, the following wound management was agreed upon to provide the best functional and cosmetic result.
Performed:
The wound was closed with a Complex Closure on the same day of mohs.
Reconstruction:
Repair: Complex Closure
The postoperative deficit was 2.1 x 1.6 cm. The wound was closed with a complex closure. The defect extended into the deep subcutaneous tissue and superficial fascia. The wound was undermined extensively (1.0 cm) to mobilize adjacent tissue, reduce wound tension and minimize the distortion of the adjacent functional structures. Buried absorbable sutures were placed to close the subcutaneous tissue and superficial fascia, followed by meticulous epidermal approximation. Closure necessitated advanced layered techniques beyond a routine intermediate repair due to the defect size, depth, and location. The wound was approximated utilizing 4-O Vicryl and 4-O Nylon sutures.
The final closure size is 4.7 cm.

 Post-Operative:
The patient tolerated the procedure well with no complications, and minimal bleeding. The wound was covered with a Press Dressing. Detailed oral and written post-operative instructions were reviewed and given to the patient. The patient instructed to remove dressing in 24 hours, sleep with the surgical site elevated this evening, avoid alcohol, trauma and tobacco.
 Patient was informed to return in 10 Days.

Location: Right Temple
Pre-op Diagnosis: Squamous Cell Carcinoma 
Pathology Reference: HC26-00263
Mohs Log: 6495
In an informative discussion of the mohs procedure, the nature of the skin cancer, 
 alternative treatment options, consequences of no treatment and convalescence were reviewed with the patient. 
 The risks and benefits, including but not limited to: bleeding, spread of infection, pain, scarring, bruising, 
 distortion of the surrounding areas, allergic reaction to the medications used, recurrence of the tumor, 
 and opening of the wound were discussed and understood by the patient. 
All questions from the patient were answered adequately. 
 No guarantees were given and the patient was informed that Mohs surgery is indicated to provide 
 the highest cure rate by excising the tissue in multiple stages and preserving the healthy tissue as much as possible. 
After obtaining informed consent, the patient was given a mirror and 
 corroborated the location of the tumor prior to surgery. 
 Mohs Micrographic Procedure:
Attention was turned toward the surgical site, which was disinfected and cleansed with alcohol. The patient was draped using a sterile technique. Local anesthetic lidocaine 1 % with epinephrine was infiltrated around the site. The procedure was performed in accordance with the Mohs micrographic surgical technique. 
 The tumor site was debulk prior to the first Mohs stage. With each stage, a thin layer of tissue was excised with a narrow margin of normal appearing skin using the Mohs fresh tissue technique. Hemostasis was achieved with Pressure, Electrocautery. The surgical site was dressed with gauze pressure dressings and the patient remained in the surgical area. The tissue was prepared for the cryostat and sectioned. Each section was coded, cut and stained for microscopic examination. The slides were then analyzed by the Mohs surgeon for any evidence of remaining tumor. Areas noted to be positive on the previous stage (if applicable) were removed with the mohs technique and processed for analysis. This process was repeated until a tumor free plane was obtained. 
1st Stage: 2 Sections, Negative
Post-Mohs Deficit Size: 1.9 x 1.6 cm.
 Repair Options:
 
 After a discussion of wound management options, 
 due to the size and location of the defect, and the need to achieve optimal cosmesis, 
 avoid hematoma, the following wound management was agreed upon to provide the best functional and cosmetic result. 
 Performed: 
 The wound was closed with a Complex Closure on the same day of mohs. 
 Reconstruction:
The wound was closed with a complex closure on 03-11-2026. The surgical defect size is 1.9 x 1.6 cm.
Complex repair was performed because the defect extended into of the deep subcutaneous tissue and superficial non-muscular fascia. Extensive wide undermining was performed to a distance of 1.9 cm (defect width perpendicular to closure is 1.9 cm) along the edge of the entire surgical defect to mobilize adjacent tissue, reduce wound tension and minimize the distortion of the adjacent functional structure.
Buried, absorbable retention sutures were placed to close the deep subcutaneous tissue and superficial non-muscular fascia. Retention suture placement was necessary: to avoid deforming, a depressed and contracted scar, anatomic distortion and functional deficit in adjacent fixed structures, injury to tendons and nerves in the area, to ensure proper range of motion, maintain function in the area, preserve the normal anatomy, reduce subcutaneous dead space to reduce the risk of hematoma, to reduce tension to skin to allow closure, and to reduce the risk of necrosis, infection and wound dehiscence.
The deep subcutaneous tissue, superficial non-muscular fascia and dermis was closed with 3-O Vicryl sutures followed by meticulous epidermal approximation.Epidermal closure was achieved with 3-O Nylon sutures.
Closure necessitated advanced layered techniques beyond a routine intermediate repair due to the defect size, depth, and location.The final closure size was 4.4 cm. 
 Post-Operative:
The patient tolerated the procedure well with no complications, and minimal bleeding. The wound was covered with Vaseline and Press Dressing. Detailed oral and written post-operative instructions were reviewed and given to the patient. The patient instructed to remove dressing in 24 hours, sleep with the surgical site elevated this evening, avoid alcohol, trauma and tobacco.
 Patient was informed to return in 3 Months.
examination: - scaly, erythematous plaque located on right temple
patientSummary:
65 years old Male patient,
